narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 42.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students' academic records, family backgrounds, and exam outcomes, focusing on who succeeds or struggles with the bar. The model's assessment hinged most heavily on three key factors from your profile: your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score. Your strong undergrad GPA was the standout positive force here, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, as it signals the kind of consistent academic strength that typically leads to success. In contrast, your Year 3 decile of 3—below the average of about 5.5—emerged as the top concern, nudging the model toward forecasting failure because lower rankings in later years often correlate with challenges on high-stakes exams like the bar. Your LSAT score of 30, notably under the average of 36.7, added further weight in that direction, reinforcing doubts about bar readiness despite your full-time student status, which is the norm for most applicants. Compared to typical students, your GPA gives you an edge many lack, but the dip in later school performance and lower LSAT pulled you into a riskier zone where first-time bar failure rates climb. While your middle family income bracket aligns with averages and didn't sway things much, these core academic signals dominated the decision. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male applicant—many in similar demographics thrive with targeted improvements—but the model prioritizes bar passage stats to protect program outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, while reflecting on what led to that Year 3 decile drop and building strategies like extra practice or tutoring to strengthen those areas. Strengthening these could flip future predictions in your favor, opening doors at other programs or upon reapplication. You're already ahead with that GPA—use it as your foundation to prepare a stronger case next time.
